messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. No internet routing, no messing with RF, nothing in the message path. It just listens, observes, and feeds back what already happened. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. If you fancy a look: https://meshrank.net Happy to answer questions or take criticism
messages.content_html: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: Morning Peter. I have spent some time attempting to build some super J poles - yep. Incredibly difficult to get right, I've made three now and none of them are on freq - too many elements to tune in one go! The single ele J pole was trivial (just done with some twin & earth core) but trying to get the delay lines and extra elements trimmed correctly has been an excercise in annoyance
messages.content_html: Morning Peter. I have spent some time attempting to build some super J poles - yep. Incredibly difficult to get right, I've made three now and none of them are on freq - too many elements to tune in one go! I'd be interested to know how well that works. Tuning is pretty hard with a few mm or so making a big difference to the return loss.

messages.author: Papfox
messages.text: Hi Everyone. I'm new to the mesh. My immediate area seems to have no coverage I can see with the cheap companion device I bought. I think I'm going to need to put in my own repeater. The two nearest repeaters I can see on the mesh map are "Lando Loft Repeater" and "M34A Repeater" but I can't see them on letsmesh. What's the best way to determine if they are connected to the wider mesh or if they're an island please?

messages.author: G7RPG - Peter - IO90LT
messages.text: Hey Jason, I have been toying with something stealth Meon Valley area or a bit toward Winchester (Cheesefoot) but I'll hang on now. Have you had a look at some traces from 67 to other key sites? I decided it would be too hard to try and make a super J based on how fiddly the single one is. Pleased I never tried now. I've got an aliexress 5.5, Paradar 6.5 and McGill 6dB. My simple J is 3~6dB better to Butser and Chillerton. A bit disappointed I wasted so much money but hey!

868Mhz 5dBi whip antenna with SMA + RP-SMA connector for LoRa, Mesh...
Free shipping via Royal Mail 48 to UK addresses The smallest of our range, our 5dBi indoor antennas are an enhanced tuned dipole design from brass tube, producing an extremely efficient response at the chosen frequency. The "old-fashioned" design is more efficient, less affected by nearby objects and interference and a

LoRa Antenna with Pigtail - 868MHz Black
This is a 170mm black 868MHz 50ohm antenna kit tested and recommended by iLabs for use with their LoRa products, such as the Challenger RP2040 LoRa. They can also be used with any other LoRa board requiring an 868MHz U.FL antenna solution.

messages.author: G7RPG - Peter - IO90LT
messages.text: 2w panel might be pushing it in the winter. Just some back of an envelope sums. Say you've got 5000mAh 50% DoC is 2500. at 8mA (no tx) that'll give you 312 hours or 13 days. Now lets add some TX time in.... I'd say and just a guess divide by 2 = 6 days with 5000mAh and no charge at all.
messages.content_html: 2w panel might be pushing it in the winter. Just some back of an envelope sums. Say you've got 5000mAh 50% DoC is 2500. at 8mA (no tx) that'll give you 312 hours or 13 days. Now lets add some TX time in.... I'd say and just a guess divide by 2 = 6 days with 5000mAh and no charge at all.

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Morning all — a few updates to MeshRank.net: Heltec V4 can now run as a combined Observer + Repeater (beta). An NTP time sync has been added once Wi-Fi connects, so the device always has the correct date/time after reboot. This also includes the Heltec V4 SX1262 / GC1109 RX fix. As this is beta, please don’t update the repeater that’s bolted to your roof just yet The MQTT feed is now open for anyone already using meshcoretomqtt (or similar) and wanting to mirror their packet stream into MeshRank. Advanced packet routing maps are nearly finished. These are based on mutual neighbour connections and can infer likely paths where there are gaps in the observed route. I’m also finalising MeshLive — a real-time visual view of the mesh, including country-wide noise floor, live routes, and packets per minute. This will be released to observers first.
messages.content_html: Morning all — a few updates to MeshRank.net: Heltec V4 can now run as a combined Observer + Repeater (beta). An NTP time sync has been added once Wi-Fi connects, so the device always has the correct date/time after reboot. This also includes the Heltec V4 SX1262 / GC1109 RX fix. As this is beta, please don’t update the repeater that’s bolted to your roof just yet The MQTT feed is now open for anyone already using meshcoretomqtt (or similar) and wanting to mirror their packet stream into MeshRank. Advanced packet routing maps are nearly finished. These are based on mutual neighbour connections and can infer likely paths where there are gaps in the observed route. I’m also finalising MeshLive — a real-time visual view of the mesh, including country-wide noise floor, live routes, and packets per minute. This will be released to observers first.

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: I fixed this issue when I was building the MeshRank repeater firmware for the Heltec V4. I’ve also added NTP lookup so once the repeater has connected to the internet it will also update the time. Fortunately it also includes MQTT so it will also become a MeshRank.net observer. No additional boxes. Simple flash, then add WiFi and MQTT key and you’re running. It’s beta at the moment but maybe worth a try?
messages.content_html: I fixed this issue when I was building the MeshRank repeater firmware for the Heltec V4. I’ve also added NTP lookup so once the repeater has connected to the internet it will also update the time. Fortunately it also includes MQTT so it will also become a MeshRank.net observer. No additional boxes. Simple flash, then add WiFi and MQTT key and you’re running. It’s beta at the moment but maybe worth a try?

messages.reply_html:
S7uart G5AIL
I fixed this issue when I was building the MeshRank repeater firmware for the Heltec V4. I’ve also added NTP lookup so once the repeater has connected to the internet it will also update the time. Fortunately it also includes MQTT so it will also become a MeshRank.net observer. No additional boxes. Simple flash, then add WiFi and MQTT key and you’re running. It’s beta at the moment but maybe worth a try?

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)
messages.content_html: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: MeshRank.net sees around 60,000 unique packets a day. Only 4,000 of those are messages. Personally I think we should embrace all messages. I don’t see any evidence of bots swamping the network. More messages will ultimately lead to more users joining. Flood adverts are the real problem. (edited)
messages.content_html: MeshRank.net sees around 60,000 unique packets a day. Only 4,000 of those are messages. Personally I think we should embrace all messages. I don’t see any evidence of bots swamping the network. More messages will ultimately lead to more users joining. Flood adverts are the real problem. (edited)

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: I’m not 100% sure. I’ve tried to discourage it in the repeater rankings. A flood advert isn’t required for the mesh to operate. It’s more for user confidence. messages.author: G7RPG - Peter - IO90LT
messages.text: Just a heads up for Solent area people. Butser Hill our main trunk route out to the wider mesh was swapped on Sunday evening under the cover of dark. Sadly the link quality has dropped massively I can only just get it from home now it's gone from +6 to -8. Only seeing a small fraction of traffic now on my observer node that reports to Meshrank and the analyzer. We really need another route to RG17 or something else along the Berkshire downs. Any suggestions? (edited)

messages.author: Luke Edwards
messages.text: In terms of updating a device up high, can you do that via a companion device, or do you have to climb up and get the device? Would an alternative be to run some wire from the device inside the loft via a short cable to the device?
messages.content_html: In terms of updating a device up high, can you do that via a companion device, or do you have to climb up and get the device? Would an alternative be to run some wire from the device inside the loft via a short cable to the device?

messages.author: mikebennett0612
messages.text: I used 20m of 12v automotive cable, and a 12v Buck converter to USBC in my case. Advantages are that you can make the cable run as long as you want, and use random DC power supplies. Updates over Wifi easy. (log on to repeater, serial , then "start ota" Creates a Wifi netowrk to join and then update.. Easy to reboot from the PSU.
messages.content_html: I used 20m of 12v automotive cable, and a 12v Buck converter to USBC in my case. Advantages are that you can make the cable run as long as you want, and use random DC power supplies. Updates over Wifi easy. (log on to repeater, serial , then "start ota" Creates a Wifi netowrk to join and then update.. Easy to reboot from the PSU.

messages.author: pir
messages.text: Though this quote probably means that it isn't needed anymore: The problem isn't turning off the LNA, the problem was the LNA wasn't being turned on after transmitting (and RX ending up in the TX path ALWAYS) The LNA doesn't need to be turned off - it needs to be switched INTO the RF path. That's what PA_TX_EN controls. When LOW, the switch routes through LNA. The fix ensures PA_TX_EN is LOW during RX.
messages.content_html: Though this quote probably means that it isn't needed anymore: The problem isn't turning off the LNA, the problem was the LNA wasn't being turned on after transmitting (and RX ending up in the TX path ALWAYS) The LNA doesn't need to be turned off - it needs to be switched INTO the RF path. That's what PA_TX_EN controls. When LOW, the switch routes through LNA. The fix ensures PA_TX_EN is LOW during RX.

That's what PA_TX_EN controls. When LOW, the switch routes through LNA. The fix ensures PA_TX_EN is LOW during RX.

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: Cool. From the comment above then there are three things here: Register patch to fix LNA switching into RX path (good, proper fix) GPIO wire-patch to force the above behaviour (temp workaround, superseeded by the register patch) LNA gain "boosting" (comes with all the usual caveats of using more LNA gain) Thanks folks for steering me through the wild forests of github pulls, it's all rather indigestible at first glance (edited)
messages.content_html:
Cool. From the comment above then there are three things here:
  • Register patch to fix LNA switching into RX path (good, proper fix)
  • GPIO wire-patch to force the above behaviour (temp workaround, superseeded by the register patch)
  • LNA gain "boosting" (comes with all the usual caveats of using more LNA gain) Thanks folks for steering me through the wild forests of github pulls, it's all rather indigestible at first glance
(edited)

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: It's all good Bryan, I'm new round these parts have entirely failed to stay on top of progress, so that's on me. I fully understand/agree with issues surrounding wide front-end gain, indeed our well-sited meshcore repeater site gets hammered by all sorts of signals right across UHF proper and the only solution to that is of course filtering.. which is always the solution to any busy RF environment anyway. As we have had to do with our ham repeater / data stations, all of which are on busy sites. Comes with the territory! I admire the efforts of everyone who has worked through the issue at a firmware level, and all the folks doing the on-air experiments. Thanks for your message!! 🫡
messages.content_html: It's all good Bryan, I'm new round these parts have entirely failed to stay on top of progress, so that's on me. I fully understand/agree with issues surrounding wide front-end gain, indeed our well-sited meshcore repeater site gets hammered by all sorts of signals right across UHF proper and the only solution to that is of course filtering.. which is always the solution to any busy RF environment anyway. As we have had to do with our ham repeater / data stations, all of which are on busy sites. Comes with the territory! I admire the efforts of everyone who has worked through the issue at a firmware level, and all the folks doing the on-air experiments. Thanks for your message!! 🫡
messages.reply_html:
Bryan#1364 (CATM)
I wish this had been handled better and was clear about what fixes had been rolled out. I'm not disputing that maybe there have been bugs found in the firmware but much of findings do not stack up with real life. There is a basic problem of taking a wideband chip with no filtering and adding an LNA and connecting it to a high gain antenna in our busy environment. Overload and rx blocking is almost inevitable. Many people found a complete cure of deafness was to fit a cavity filter or in one case to simply remove the LNA with a wire bridge. If the issue was down to these FW fixes these two sensible fixes for an overloaded receiver would not have fixed the issue so just bare in mind it's there's a lot more involved. No-one has mentioned the G2 which suffers the same or worse deafness issues due to an even higher gain LNA not seen anyone rolling out these FW fixes for the G2

messages.author: Luke Edwards
messages.text: If I was to get something like this to route the antenna through, would it weaken the signal? https://paradar.co.uk/products/ultra-low-loss-x-400-cable-n-male-to-rp-sma-male-15m
messages.content_html: If I was to get something like this to route the antenna through, would it weaken the signal? https://paradar.co.uk/products/ultra-low-loss-x-400-cable-n-male-to-rp-sma-male-15m
embeds_html:
Ultra-low-loss X-400 cable, N-male to RP-SMA male, 15m
LMR-400 cable is about the best cable you can buy for use with frequencies between 500Mhz and 3Ghz. It's the bees knees! The ultra low attenuation of 1.30dB per metre between DC and 3GHz means that almost no signal is lost in the cable. We recommend this cable for Helium / HNT mining (and other 868Mhz applications!).

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: It will always attentuate to some extent, here's the datasheet https://timesmicrowave.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/lmr-400-datasheet-1.pdf Looking at 12.8dB/100m, so for 15m about 2dB signal loss
messages.content_html: It will always attentuate to some extent, here's the datasheet https://timesmicrowave.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/lmr-400-datasheet-1.pdf Looking at 12.8dB/100m, so for 15m about 2dB signal loss

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: Legends. Can I recommend going to town waterproofing the connection between the antenna and the coax? A dab of silicone grease on the female-N side, then once its attached a continuous spiral of leccy tape (3M Temflex ideally) to completely encapsulate everything all the way down to the coax, then on top of that a layer of self-amalgamating tape to protect the PVC
messages.content_html: Legends. Can I recommend going to town waterproofing the connection between the antenna and the coax? A dab of silicone grease on the female-N side, then once its attached a continuous spiral of leccy tape (3M Temflex ideally) to completely encapsulate everything all the way down to the coax, then on top of that a layer of self-amalgamating tape to protect the PVC
messages.reactions_html: 1

embeds_html:
LoRa 4MHz-wide Cavity Filter
A premium LoRa 900MHz-related band cavity filter that provides low-loss filtering of 900-915 MHz LoRa signals between an antenna and a LoRa device. Increases signal SNR, reduces out of band harmonics, and allows for the weaker and distant nodes to be heard. The Airframes LoRa Cavity Filter is Ideal for Meshtastic, Mesh

embeds_html:
868 MHz Band Pass Filter - WS Kommunikation
868 MHz 50 Watts Band Pass Filter The FP-868 is a 3-cavity band-pass filter using full-length quarter-wave resonators. The filter can be tuned within the ISM band, 865-870 MHz band. Careful design and choice of materials ensure reliable operation over a wide temperature range

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: Well it's much tighter than the SAW - with it I should hopefully be able to attenuate the other signals on 865-867, saw is wide open 863-877. The cavity from the factory is the same width-ish as the SAW, and the insertion loss is 1.3dB instead of 2.6dB for the SAW. So in stock form, yeah, SAW is far better value for money. Tuned, it could be... priceless?!

messages.text: Some of you may have seen in the public chat. Happy to report I have completed hardware and software for a cross-mesh TC2 BBS setup. 1x MT and 1x MC node feeding into the same BBS. To access it you DM the node. Node is currently called M0LIH MC/MT BBS (will shorten that later). Public key : fe63e711afac02bf4df518b175b96b52366f686bbf01e5f2b443cbdfaf51141a Hardware is not in final, elevated, location yet, so results may vary.

messages.author: mikebennett0612
messages.text: So - my musings on the new 1.14 firmware on the V4s. Having driven around this weekend as part of my dads taxi duties, in the same areas as previous, i think that the new firmware gives slightly better range (using https://mapme.sh/) . This is obviously not very scientific, there are many variables (not least that my V4 repeater is in testing up a temp 10m pole at the moment) but to me - it feels better. Getting responses in hexes that i have visited over 10 times previous without response, and just generally finding more while out and about driving. I am still wondering if the update has had a play in the reduction of incoming messages to my repeater (gone from around 8/s to now around 2/sec - but i am not convinced on that yet, as the reduction was apparent before the update by about 12 hours (though this could be due to the repeater or other repeaters feeding me potentially being updated to 1.14) I have 2 SAW filters due for delivery this week ( https://www.aliexpress.com/item/1005007121506777.html?spm=a2g0o.order_list.order_list_main.4.5afa1802dSQGaa) - so will put them on both v4s and repeat and analyse. In short, I think its better, but no scientific proof, i have no background in RF (IT for the last 25 years), so take my ramblings with a pinch of salt. Still yet to see the UoS repeater which is a bit concerning, I would have expected to having LOS and around 9km distance. Cheers Meshers!!!

messages.author: G7RPG - Peter - IO90LT
messages.text: The filter is a last resort really. It will either help if you have lots of very close strong signals. If your out in the sticks away from any other transmitters it'll make the receiver worse, 3dB which is half of your power gone. The comprimose you'll have to make is wasting the V4's extra power output. (edited)
messages.content_html: The filter is a last resort really. It will either help if you have lots of very close strong signals. If your out in the sticks away from any other transmitters it'll make the receiver worse, 3dB which is half of your power gone. The comprimose you'll have to make is wasting the V4's extra power output. (edited)

Getting responses in hexes that i have visited over 10 times previous without response, and just generally finding more while out and about driving. I am still wondering if the update has had a play in the reduction of incoming messages to my repeater (gone from around 8/s to now around 2/sec - but i am not convinced on that yet, as the reduction was apparent before the update by about 12 hours (though this could be due to the repeater or other repeaters feeding me potentially being updated to 1.14) I have 2 SAW filters due for delivery this week (https://www.aliexpress.com/item/1005007121506777.html?spm=a2g0o.order_list.order_list_main.4.5afa1802dSQGaa) - so will put them on both v4s and repeat and analyse. In short, I think its better, but no scientific proof, i have no background in RF (IT for the last 25 years), so take my ramblings with a pinch of salt. Still yet to see the UoS repeater which is a bit concerning, I would have expected to having LOS and around 9km distance. Cheers Meshers!!!

messages.author: mking834
messages.text: I have set my Seed sensecap solar node up, not yet put it in it's final position, but it seems to be working. When I look at my neighbours on meshrank, some are set to "Mutual" others to "Incoming only" which is what mine is set to, do I need to change this.
messages.content_html: I have set my Seed sensecap solar node up, not yet put it in it's final position, but it seems to be working. When I look at my neighbours on meshrank, some are set to "Mutual" others to "Incoming only" which is what mine is set to, do I need to change this.

messages.author: Rob
messages.text: So.. The original trial Butser 93 was replaced by 7e with a fake alpha antenna but you & I felt it performed worse. I went back to screw on a mcgill instead and made a mess of the case so threw 93 back up with shrink wrap to try and waterproof the antenna a little. It's still there now but repeats turned off. 7e was rebuilt with a 3dbi mcgill and placed about 8m away, found the best spot that I knew could receive RG17. I plan to retrieve 93 and just leave 7e in place. (edited)
messages.content_html: So.. The original trial Butser 93 was replaced by 7e with a fake alpha antenna but you & I felt it performed worse. I went back to screw on a mcgill instead and made a mess of the case so threw 93 back up with shrink wrap to try and waterproof the antenna a little. It's still there now but repeats turned off. 7e was rebuilt with a 3dbi mcgill and placed about 8m away, found the best spot that I knew could receive RG17. I plan to retrieve 93 and just leave 7e in place. (edited)

messages.author: jburgess777
messages.text: I don’t think it is a setting but is derived from the observed message paths Incoming only = you received messages from them mutual = you received messages from them, and they received message from you
messages.content_html:
I don’t think it is a setting but is derived from the observed message paths
  • Incoming only = you received messages from them
  • mutual = you received messages from them, and they received message from you
messages.reply_html:
mking834
I have set my Seed sensecap solar node up, not yet put it in it's final position, but it seems to be working. When I look at my neighbours on meshrank, some are set to "Mutual" others to "Incoming only" which is what mine is set to, do I need to change this.

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: Morning folks Regarding the adverts, most mesh traffic is flood ads and in busy areas it's starting to cripple the network, so I the recommendation is that less is more - I have our boxes set to 72 hours. Zero hop ads are more useful for local users and don't congest the mesh, so 12 hours seems appropriate.
messages.content_html: Morning folks Regarding the adverts, most mesh traffic is flood ads and in busy areas it's starting to cripple the network, so I the recommendation is that less is more - I have our boxes set to 72 hours. Zero hop ads are more useful for local users and don't congest the mesh, so 12 hours seems appropriate.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Jason G5RKT IO90hw
messages.text: That wild card is just the rule to handle scope-less packets. Without any scopes set up then all packets are repeated regardless of scope by a repeater, once you start adding scopes then scope-less packets, and packets with a defined scope, are passed and everything else is dropped.
messages.content_html: That wild card is just the rule to handle scope-less packets. Without any scopes set up then all packets are repeated regardless of scope by a repeater, once you start adding scopes then scope-less packets, and packets with a defined scope, are passed and everything else is dropped.
messages.reply_html:
Colz
Hmm makes me wonder if the wildcard overrides them (edited)

messages.author: typhoon_9978
messages.text: Hi all! I'm very new to meshcore, you might have seen me in #public and #test. Thank you if you've replied and apologies for the wall of text! I have become instantly addicted and I'm absolutely clueless I'm learning as I go, building a repeater (currently HobbitonV4), gradually converting my free money into radio equipment . I'm a bit stuck as I'm out on the edge a bit and I could do with some help. My only reliable gateway to the wider mesh is "so'ton west omni", which I reckon is true for the area. It's most likely getting spammed to bits. What I'd like to do is increase the available paths onto the mesh, and improve things in the New Forest direction if I can. Is it as "easy" as taking a see(ee)eeed P1-pro up to Tatchbury Mount somewhere? I'd rather coordinate with others if there are better ways of doing it... Very happy to RTFM, if people can point me in the direction of a FM that's half decent, there's a lot I don't know. I don't even know what my questions are!

messages.author: typhoon_9978
messages.text: Interesting morning. I took a 2db stick antenna up Tatchbury Mount. The goal being to see if I can find a northern route out of the western side of the solent. Pretty much all traffic hit either my repeaters I have at home, soton west omni or it started picking up IoW repeaters. to me this didn't really count as often the IoW repeaters are second hops to soton west omni, also, it's not north so its not the goal. The North side of Tatchbury Mount was dark. Unfortunately something of the right size to get further north to Romsey would be hard to hide and expensive to lose. We could probably do with something in the Broadlands Estate or Ower Macdonalds or Hayes Music. It'd be grand to put something there that can be maintained.

messages.author: djembejim
messages.text: Something to consider. IOW also has a route north by Buster that hits my repeater directly in Wiltshire. IOW to near Marlborough is 4 hops and pretty solid. The south of Salisbury repeater Stand Lynch Hill hits Morgan’s hill repeater to the north so there are numerous routes south to north. more is better though
messages.content_html: Something to consider. IOW also has a route north by Buster that hits my repeater directly in Wiltshire. IOW to near Marlborough is 4 hops and pretty solid. The south of Salisbury repeater Stand Lynch Hill hits Morgan’s hill repeater to the north so there are numerous routes south to north. more is better though
messages.reply_html:
typhoon_9978
Interesting morning. I took a 2db stick antenna up Tatchbury Mount. The goal being to see if I can find a northern route out of the western side of the solent. Pretty much all traffic hit either my repeaters I have at home, soton west omni or it started picking up IoW repeaters. to me this didn't really count as often the IoW repeaters are second hops to soton west omni, also, it's not north so its not the goal. The North side of Tatchbury Mount was dark. Unfortunately something of the right size to get further north to Romsey would be hard to hide and expensive to lose. We could probably do with something in the Broadlands Estate or Ower Macdonalds or Hayes Music. It'd be grand to put something there that can be maintained.

messages.author: typhoon_9978
messages.text: Yeah we need more routes north. Here is a terrible map. Everything from the West of Southampton comes through Soton W, then to IoW, then back up to Butser. Which isn't bad, but its just one route.
messages.content_html: Yeah we need more routes north. Here is a terrible map. Everything from the West of Southampton comes through Soton W, then to IoW, then back up to Butser. Which isn't bad, but its just one route.

messages.author: djembejim
messages.text: One thing I’d say about repeaters is make them as road accessible as possible. When there’s a hike and a couple of hours round trip to update or do maintenance they become a bit of a ballache. If they really have to be out in the boonies, then a solid solar battery setup is key. @IanT [IT-Orp-S omni] has a great pcb that has a supervisor IC onboard so no more brown out hikes to go and reboot stuff
messages.content_html: One thing I’d say about repeaters is make them as road accessible as possible. messages.author: IanT [IT-Orp-S omni]
messages.text: I rolled my own firmware for that... 1) WiFi connected node that logs into the repeater (it can cope with 1-2 hops, more if the mesh is reliable) and links to mqtt 2) mqtt server (broker) on a machine running NodeRed, which takes the data and squirts it out to ThingSpeak. The Web panels are a combination of NodeRed (gauges) and Thingspeak (plots).
messages.content_html: I rolled my own firmware for that... 1) WiFi connected node that logs into the repeater (it can cope with 1-2 hops, more if the mesh is reliable) and links to mqtt 2) mqtt server (broker) on a machine running NodeRed, which takes the data and squirts it out to ThingSpeak. The Web panels are a combination of NodeRed (gauges) and Thingspeak (plots).
